summary: I was born in Brownsville, Texas, on October 16th, 1923, to Benita Casarez and Jose Alvarez. Brownsville is where my early life began before I joined the Navy. It holds a special place in my heart as my hometown and where my family roots are.

San Benito, Texas, is closely connected to my early years alongside Brownsville. Though I was born in Brownsville, San Benito was also part of my upbringing and local area. It’s part of the region where my family lived and where I grew up before my naval service.

I did my boot camp at the Naval Training Center in San Diego. It was tough but necessary to prepare me for my service during World War II and beyond. The training there shaped me into the sailor I became, ready to serve on the USS Tennessee.

I served in World War II from 1941 to 1946 aboard the USS Tennessee. I participated in 13 major battles across the Pacific Theater, including the tough Aleutian Islands campaign. It was a time of intense combat, burials at sea, and camaraderie with shipmates like J.D. Wilson. The war shaped much of my life and naval career.

During the Korean War, I was stationed in France where we built an air base. My role was mainly maintenance, but I also served as military police because I could speak some French. It was a different experience from World War II, focusing more on support and construction.

I served two tours in the Vietnam War, where I was exposed to Agent Orange on the Mekong River. I was handling salvage boats, retrieving small craft damaged by the Viet Cong. It was a dangerous time, and sadly, my friend Marty Martinez was killed by the Viet Cong during that war.

I served aboard the USS Tennessee, a battleship, from 1941 to 1946 during World War II. We traveled all over the Pacific Theater, engaging in 13 major battles. Life on the Tennessee was tough but rewarding, with plenty of supplies, entertainment from shipmates like J.D. Wilson, and close friendships that lasted a lifetime.

entity: Pacific Theater of World War II
summary: The Pacific Theater was where I saw most of my combat during World War II aboard the USS Tennessee. We operated from the Philippine Sea to the Aleutian Islands, down to the tip of South America, and even Japan. It was a vast area with many battles, including the difficult Aleutian campaign.

The Philippine Sea was one of the key locations where the USS Tennessee operated during World War II. We fought in major battles there as part of the Pacific Theater campaigns. It was a strategic area that saw intense naval combat.

The Aleutian Islands battle was the toughest I faced during World War II. The enemy was well entrenched, and it took a lot of firepower to take them out. Serving there was a harsh experience but one of my most memorable moments aboard the USS Tennessee.

Our travels aboard the USS Tennessee during World War II took us as far south as the tip of South America. It was part of the wide range of locations in the Pacific Theater where we operated. Seeing so many ports and places was one of the unique aspects of my naval service.

Japan was one of the countries we operated near during World War II in the Pacific Theater. The USS Tennessee was involved in battles close to Japan as part of the effort to defeat the enemy. It was a critical area in the final phases of the war.

During World War II, we faced kamikaze attacks aboard the USS Tennessee. These suicide attacks caused casualties and damage, including hits to our ship. It was a terrifying but defining part of the combat experience in the Pacific.

J.D. Wilson was a fellow shipmate on the USS Tennessee and quite a character. He was an entertainer who did imitations and even made home brew in the gun locker, which once exploded and caused quite a scene. He helped keep morale up during the tough times at sea.

J.D. Wilson, the entertainer on the USS Tennessee, said he was from Tacoma, Washington. I remember that because he was a memorable character during our time at sea. Tacoma was his hometown, which he mentioned occasionally.

After I was discharged in 1972, I walked out of the 32nd Street Naval Station in San Diego. It was near there that I found work at the National Steel and Shipbuilding Company, staying close to the ships I loved. The station marked the transition from military to civilian life for me.

After leaving the Navy, I worked at the National Steel and Shipbuilding Company in San Diego. It was fitting to continue working with ships, building them after years of sailing. This job helped me adjust to civilian life while staying connected to the maritime world.

Marty Martinez was a close friend and fellow Boatswain's Mate who shared a bunk with me on the USS Tennessee. Sadly, he was killed in Vietnam shortly after being transferred there, blasted out of the water by the Viet Cong. His loss was deeply felt among us shipmates.

The Viet Cong were the enemy during my Vietnam War service. They blew up small craft we salvaged on the Mekong River and were responsible for killing my friend Marty Martinez. Facing them was dangerous, and their tactics made the war very challenging.

We have annual USS Tennessee reunions where shipmates gather to remember our service. We hosted one here in the mid-1980s with about 400 people attending for a week. The reunions move around the country, with recent ones held in places like New Orleans, though I haven't been able to attend in the last ten years due to illness.

New Orleans was the location of the USS Tennessee reunion last month in 2011. Unfortunately, I haven't been able to attend reunions like this one for the past ten years because of my health issues. It’s one of the many places where we gather to honor our shared naval history.

I was treated at Valley Regional Medical Center in 2000 after a heart valve replacement went wrong and I lost a lot of blood. The doctors and nurses there saved my life when I went into a coma and suffered multiple complications. It was a critical part of my recovery journey.

After complications at Valley Regional, I was airlifted to Methodist Hospital in Houston for skin graft surgery and to close my chest wounds. They managed to get me through a very difficult period, including treating infections and pneumonia. Methodist Hospital was crucial in my long recovery.

During my Vietnam War service on the Mekong River, I was exposed to Agent Orange when planes sprayed the foliage to clear it. The chemical contaminated everything, and I breathed it in and had it on me. It was a dangerous exposure that many veterans like me suffered from.

I served on the Mekong River during the Vietnam War, man-handling salvage boats to recover small craft damaged by the Viet Cong. It was a hazardous environment with constant threats, including exposure to Agent Orange. The Mekong was a key location in my Vietnam experience.

During the Korean War, I was stationed in France where we built an air base. I did maintenance work and served as military police because I spoke some French. It was a unique assignment far from the front lines but important for the war effort.
